evals.prompt: I'm thinking about launching a referral program. Where do I start?
evals.expected_output: Pulls existing channel mix and cohort LTV to baseline current referral volume before designing a program. Walks through double-sided vs one-sided structural decision, reward shape options, eligibility windows, and trigger placement. Should not jump to a specific reward magnitude without baseline data.
assertions: Calls modern.sales.by_channel to baseline current referral volume
Calls modern.retention.cohort_ltv to size LTV gap for referred customers
Walks through double-sided vs one-sided structural decision
Discusses reward shape options (cash, credit, product, percentage)
Discusses eligibility windows (order count, time, product usage, NPS)
Discusses trigger placement options
Output has Quick Wins, High-Impact, Test Ideas sections

evals.prompt: Should I do double-sided rewards or just reward the referrer?
evals.expected_output: Walks through the double-sided vs one-sided math. Default to double-sided for typical DTC; one-sided sometimes outperforms for high-AOV considered-purchase categories. Cites typical participation lift (3% one-sided vs 9-14% double-sided). Asks about the catalog before defaulting.
assertions: Defaults to double-sided for typical DTC ecomm
Names one-sided exception for high-AOV considered-purchase categories
Cites participation-rate lift of double-sided vs one-sided
Asks about the catalog and AOV before defaulting
Acknowledges per-acquisition cost is higher but volume difference compensates
Output has Quick Wins, High-Impact, Test Ideas sections

evals.prompt: Should the referrer reward be cash, store credit, or a free product?
evals.expected_output: Walks through the reward shape framework. Default to store credit for ecomm; product reward for catalogs that support it; avoid cash. Notes that referrer-side and friend-side rewards do not need to match in shape. Recommends store credit + friend-side percentage as a common high-performance structure.
assertions: Recommends store credit as the default ecomm referrer reward
Names product reward as the high-leverage move for catalogs that support it
Warns against cash unless the brand has a specific reason
Notes referrer-side and friend-side rewards can differ in shape
Recommends store-credit + friend-percentage as a common structure
Output has Quick Wins, High-Impact, Test Ideas sections

evals.prompt: How do I prevent people from referring themselves with a second email?
evals.expected_output: Walks through the fraud-resistance framework. Lists self-referral, address stuffing, credit stacking, code-marketplace leakage, and employee abuse. Recommends specific detection signals: shared payment method, shared device fingerprint, shared shipping address. Names the typical 15-30% fraud rate on poorly designed programs.
assertions: Names self-referral as a primary fraud pattern
Lists detection signals (shared payment, device fingerprint, shipping address)
Names address-stuffing pattern
Names credit-stacking pattern with refund clawback recommendation
Names code-marketplace leakage as a separate risk
Cites the 15-30% fraud rate on poorly designed programs
Output has Quick Wins, High-Impact, Test Ideas sections

evals.prompt: When should I ask customers to refer? On the order confirmation page?
evals.expected_output: Walks through the post-purchase trigger placement framework. Order confirmation is the wrong primary trigger (customer has no verdict). Recommends post-validation window (day 7-21), post-second-order, and CS-resolved-positive interactions as higher-quality placements. Recommends multiple triggers rather than one.
assertions: Identifies order confirmation as the wrong primary trigger
Recommends post-validation window (day 7-21) as a high-quality placement
Recommends post-second-order as the highest-intent placement
Names CS-resolved-positive interaction as a capture surface
Recommends multiple triggers rather than one
Output has Quick Wins, High-Impact, Test Ideas sections

evals.prompt: What's a good viral coefficient for an ecomm referral program?
evals.expected_output: Defines viral coefficient K = (% who refer) x (referrals per referrer) x (conversion of referrals). Notes typical DTC band is K = 0.05 to 0.25 and that K = 1.0 is essentially never reached in ecomm. Walks through the lever ordering (conversion-of-referrals first, participation second, referrals-per-referrer last).
assertions: Defines viral coefficient K formula
Names typical DTC ecomm band of K = 0.05 to 0.25
Notes K = 1.0 is rarely reached in ecomm
Names conversion-of-referrals as highest-leverage component
Names participation rate as second-highest leverage component
Names referrals-per-referrer as smallest lever
Output has Quick Wins, High-Impact, Test Ideas sections

evals.prompt: How does the referrer's own LTV change when they refer someone?
evals.expected_output: Names the referrer-LTV uplift mechanism. Typical 15-35% repeat-rate uplift in referrers vs matched non-referrers. Explains the implication for design: maximize the number of one-time referrers rather than the average referrals per referrer. Pulls cohort LTV grouped by referrer status to validate.
assertions: Names the referrer-LTV uplift mechanism
Cites typical 15-35% repeat-rate uplift
Explains the design implication (maximize one-time referrers)
Recommends low-friction first-referral prompts over high-payout repeat-incentive structures
Calls modern.retention.cohort_ltv grouped by referrer status to validate
Output has Quick Wins, High-Impact, Test Ideas sections

evals.prompt: I only have 600 active buyers. Can I run a referral program?
evals.expected_output: Notes that under 1,000 active buyers makes a broad program theoretical because the pool is too small for the math to work. Recommends shifting toward seeding a founding-cohort referral instead. Discusses high-touch referrer onboarding for the early-customer set rather than a generic program. References subscription-growth founding-cohort framework if applicable.
assertions: Identifies under 1,000 active buyers as too small for a broad program
Recommends founding-cohort referral seeding instead
Recommends high-touch referrer onboarding for the early set
Does not recommend a standard double-sided program at this scale
References subscription-growth founding-cohort if applicable
Output has Quick Wins, High-Impact, Test Ideas sections
